1: The theme of spiritual death reversed only by Christ: "dead in your transgressions and sins" names the condition from which the new life of Ephesians 2:5-6 rescues. Ezekiel's valley of dry bones (Ezekiel 37:3) is the prophetic background — dead Israel waiting for the breath of God. Paul universalizes that national death into the individual condition of every human being apart from Christ's quickening. Only the one who himself rose from the dead can give life to those who are dead.
2: A revelation of God: the "ruler of the kingdom of the air" — the cosmic power hostile to God that governs the pattern of this age — is named so that its defeat by Christ can be understood. Colossians 2:15 makes explicit what Ephesians implies: Christ "disarmed the rulers and authorities, triumphing over them by the cross." The Ephesians were once subject to this ruler; they are now under a different lordship — the one who defeated that power.
3: The theme of universal human guilt that makes Christ's rescue necessary: "by nature deserving of wrath" names the starting point from which grace rescues. Paul includes "all of us" — Jew and Gentile alike — in this condition, grounding the need for Christ in the universal corruption of human nature, not merely in ignorance or circumstance. The wrath is not arbitrary but the just response of a holy God to a creation in full rebellion.
4: A revelation of God enacted in Christ: "because of his great love for us, God, who is rich in mercy, made us alive." The mercy that motivates the resurrection of the dead is the same hesed that God announced at Sinai (Exodus 34:6) — steadfast, abundant, loyal love. The cross is the decisive demonstration of that love: the Son becomes the object of the wrath that sinners deserved so that sinners may receive the life the Son deserves.
5: A direct Christological claim: "made us alive with Christ" — the spiritual resurrection of believers is inseparable from Christ's own resurrection. To be made alive is to be united to the Living One. The parenthetical "(it is by grace you have been saved)" insists that the rescue has no human contribution: the dead do not cooperate with their own resurrection. The saving grace operates through Christ's death and resurrection, applied by the Spirit.
6: A direct Christological claim: believers are proleptically seated "with him in the heavenly realms in Christ Jesus." The exaltation described in Ephesians 1:20 — Christ at the Father's right hand — is shared with those who are in him. This is the "already" of eschatological salvation: the position is secured by Christ's ascension even though its full experiential reality awaits the end. Believers participate now in his victory, not merely await it.
7: The theme of Christ as the eternal demonstration of God's grace: "in the coming ages he might show the incomparable riches of his grace, expressed in his kindness to us in Christ Jesus." The church — those rescued from death and seated in Christ — is the everlasting exhibit of what divine grace accomplished in the cross. The age to come will not forget what Christ did; it will display it forever.
8: A direct statement of the gospel's Christological structure: "by grace through faith" — grace as the source (what God does in Christ), faith as the means (how the human receives it). The gift is not faith itself as an independent virtue but the entire salvation, which arrives through faith. The boast-excluding logic of the verse runs on Christ's completed work: there is nothing left for human achievement to contribute.
9: The theme of works-exclusion grounded in Christ's sufficiency: "not by works, so that no one can boast." The abolition of boasting is not an ethic about humility; it is a Christological consequence. If Christ has done everything — died, risen, become our righteousness (1 Corinthians 1:30) — then there is nothing remaining for human accomplishment to add. Any works-supplement would reintroduce boasting and deny the cross.
10: A fulfillment of Genesis 1:26-28: believers are "God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works." The image of God in which Adam was made equipped him for the stewardship of creation; sin fractured that image and frustrated the vocation. The new creation in Christ restores the image (explicitly stated in Ephesians 4:24) and with it the capacity and calling to do the works "God prepared in advance." The good works are not the basis of salvation but the purpose of those already saved.
11: The theme of historical division that Christ's cross resolves: the former Gentile condition ("uncircumcised," outside the covenant community) is recalled so that the reconciliation of Ephesians 2:13-16 stands out in full relief. The circumcision/uncircumcision boundary was not arbitrary but was the visible mark of the covenant distinction God established with Abraham. Christ's death creates the one new humanity that neither circumcision nor uncircumcision can constitute.
12: The theme of the covenants as the trajectory toward Christ: Gentiles were "foreigners to the covenants of the promise" — the Abrahamic, Mosaic, and Davidic covenants that together constituted the long preparation for Christ. To be outside those covenants was to be outside the line along which God's redemptive plan was moving. Christ is the fulfillment of all those covenants; in him, those who were outside gain the inheritance the covenants promised.
13: A direct fulfillment: "brought near by the blood of Christ." The Levitical blood-offering made approach to God possible within the bounded space of the tabernacle — proximity purchased by sacrifice. Christ's blood accomplishes the same on a universal and permanent scale. Those "far away" (the Gentiles of Isaiah 57:19) are brought near not by taking up residence in Israel but by being included in the covenant community that Christ's blood inaugurates.
14: A direct fulfillment: "he himself is our peace" (Isaiah 9:6 — "Prince of Peace"; Isaiah 57:19 — "peace to the far and near"). Christ does not merely bring peace as an ambassador; he is peace — the embodied resolution of both human-divine and human-human enmity. The dividing wall between the inner and outer courts of the Jerusalem temple (which barred Gentiles, on pain of death) was the physical sign of the cosmic hostility that Christ's body abolished.
15: A direct Christological claim: Christ abolishes "in his flesh the law with its commands and regulations" — not annulling the law's moral content but ending its function as the wall that separated Jew from Gentile and separated both from God. The "one new humanity" created out of the two is the beginning of the new creation (Isaiah 65:17) in which the old ethnic and covenantal boundaries are transcended. Christ's body on the cross is the place where the old order ends.
16: A direct Christological claim: "reconcile both of them to God through the cross, by which he put to death their hostility." The cross achieves a double reconciliation — vertical (humanity to God) and horizontal (Jew to Gentile) — in a single act. The Greek places "the cross" and "in him" in structural parallel: the instrument of reconciliation and the person of reconciliation are inseparable. The enmity that separated creation from its Creator is put to death where Christ himself was put to death.
17: A fulfillment: "He came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near" quotes or closely echoes Isaiah 57:19 and Isaiah 52:7. The herald whose feet are beautiful, who brings good news of peace (Isaiah 52:7), is now identified with Christ himself. The incarnation is the coming of the messenger; the cross and resurrection are the peace he proclaims. He does not send word of peace; he is the word.
18: A direct Christological claim: "through him we both have access to the Father by one Spirit." The torn veil (Matthew 27:51) is the visible sign that Christ's death opened the way into the Most Holy Place — access to the Father that the entire Levitical system could only approximate. Now Jew and Gentile share this access in equal measure, through the single mediator (1 Timothy 2:5) who is both human and divine.
19: A shadow fulfilled: Israel was "God's household" (oikos tou theou — Numbers 12:7, applied to Moses), the family within which God was known as Father. Christ has expanded the household: Gentiles are now "fellow citizens" and "members of God's household" — not by joining Israel but by being incorporated into the community gathered around Israel's Messiah. Citizenship and kinship in the covenant community now come through Christ alone.
20: A direct fulfillment: Christ as "the chief cornerstone" fulfills both Isaiah 28:16 (the tested, precious stone laid in Zion that does not disappoint those who trust it) and Psalms 118:22 (the stone the builders rejected that becomes the capstone — cited by Jesus himself in Matthew 21:42 and by Peter in 1 Peter 2:7 as a direct Christological fulfillment). The new temple is built on a foundation that cannot be removed because the cornerstone has been vindicated by resurrection.
21: A shadow moving toward fulfillment: the Solomonic temple was the high point of the OT dwelling-with-God trajectory — from the Tabernacle through the Temple to Ezekiel's visionary temple (Ezek 40-48). The church as a "holy temple in the Lord" is neither Solomon's physical structure nor Ezekiel's future vision but their fulfillment: God dwells among his people not in stone but in persons joined to Christ and to one another. The building still grows (present participle) — the temple is not yet complete.
22: A direct fulfillment: "a dwelling in which God lives by his Spirit." The Shekinah-glory that filled the Tabernacle at its completion (Exodus 40:34-35) and the Temple at its dedication (1 Kings 8:10-11) now dwells in the Spirit-inhabited community of Christ. The promise of Leviticus 26:11-12 — "I will put my dwelling place among you... I will be your God and you will be my people" — is realized not through a building but through believers indwelt by the Spirit whom Christ sent.
